replies.author: @DeanTheLaughingMann
replies.text:
@AfroDan9000 The shows have distinct focuses. The Next Generation is a continuation of TOS, which focused mainly on exploring space and finding new worlds, species, and technologies. Deep Space 9 is comparable to Babylon 5, where it sets things in a specific location, and is a network for numerous alien species to come there and congregate or travel. And Voyager is a fish-out-of-water story where a whole starship and its crew wind up in an unknown part of space and work to get back home.

TNG's strength was in exploring philosophical and technical stories, as you put it. It had a rough start during its first couple of seasons but got a lot better over time. But it was an adventure story, at its core.

DS9 was a great show from start to finish but got undervalued by fans at the time because it went darker, more serious, and wasn't as adventurous as TNG. Yet it became far more appreciated over time. Like Babylon 5, it had its share of episodic stories and then stepped into an overarching story-line that built slowly over the series. However, some regard it as being where Star Trek became storytelling peak.

VOY, however...it had a good concept, some good stories, and the characters were all good. But it thrived more on its unique concept to stand as a solid enough show. It's regarded as perhaps being the weakest of the 90s Trek shows.

Enterprise is the last Star Trek show under the 'old guard'. It had a rough start, got cancelled, got revived and managed to get better after the revival. Only it still got cancelled again later. Yet it has some good elements to it.

The moment that Abrams came in with the Kelvin timeline movies is when things shifted to the Kurtzman era. But what I would say is the key difference between classic Trek and new Trek is that the former has substance over style, but the latter has style over substance.

@SnoopReddogg Just spent the last hour researching into this.  I already had the bias about DEI probably being the cause but I wanted to confirm it without just assuming.  I very much confirmed it.

Lesbian DEI hire.  New Zealand administration dodged the criticism by claiming that anyone who attacked the female captain were misogynists.  But extensive investigation confirmed it was indeed human error the was primarily the fault of the female captain, and 2 other individuals underneath her that was all a result of a lack of training and qualifications for the appointment of the position.

And another female captain of a ship ended up crashing another vessel later that same year.  This one didn't sink though and the damage wasn't nearly as bad.  The one that did sink was apparently also the first Navy ship from NZ to sink since WW2.

Asked a close friend of mine who served in the US Navy how common it is for Naval ships to be crashed in any way just incase there isn't a pattern to be drawn from the gender of these captains.  He told me that Navy vessels and their attendants go through extremely rigorous training to ensure stuff like this can never happen.  If something happens, it's either because of faulty machinery, or because someone lacked in their qualifications.

It's both depressing and a little insane to have many of these biases all validated to such an extent from just 1 little hour of intense research.  DEI is truly a cancer.

I think it’s more likely that the people in power were simply guilted into supporting the ideology of DEI, like most people who buy into it, and just acted accordingly.

They likely saw an opportunity for positive media coverage, rushed through some of the processes like ensuring someone was experienced enough or fully qualified, essentially taking a gamble with these DEI promotions, and just hoped that things would work out while garnering the positives that came with the virtue signaling in the media on the subjects. 

DEI is poison. Doesn’t matter to me if the initial intent is good. The results and nature of it all is terrible.  If people want to push the motivations of something like DEI, they need to start working from the foundation to build their diversified candidates up from the beginning instead of just placing these candidates into positions they haven’t earned or aren’t qualified for at the expense of others who objectively deserve the positions more or who are just more qualified. 

The push needs to start in the families and the elementary schools. Not at the college level or career level.

replies.author: @mattyboyanderson
replies.text:
@data_abort this is why I will always defend the concept of Midi-chlorians. It was the perfect shorthand to get the audience to understand that Anakin, even as a boy, had more force potential in his cells than even Yoda. And Palpatine knew that Obi-Wan wouldn't be able to bring himself to kill Anakin, but would instead _remove the rest of his limbs_, thereby seriously diminishing his Force potential by making less of his corporeal body. Meanwhile, the legend of Darth Vader had already struck terror into the galaxy, through his actions when he was close to full power (the massacre on Coruscant). So Palpatine has no problem controlling Vader and keeping him in line, because he's more powerful. This also makes Vader's defeat of Palpatine in ROTJ all the more poignant, because he's considerably less powerful than he was as a boy. Honestly, I find the whole Midi-chlorian aspect to be absolutely brilliant, for the reasons I've described here. 
Granted, I enjoy Dragon Ball Z, which used a similar conceit in the power scouters, allowing the audience an easy way to grasp the differences in character power levels.

author: @Pedigru
text:
ST TOS Pilot:: A grueling conversation on Slavery and the desperation driving a race of Aliens who capture Captain Pike to be a breeder.

ST TNG Pilot: Intro of a godlike race who watches as Captain Picard unravels a mystery of a colony using another alien race as a slave.

ST DSN Pilot: Sisko begins duty on DS9, tasked to build confidence in a defeated people. Meanwhile, Sisko meets an alien race and teaches them about linear life.

ST VOY Pilot: A Dying Entity pulls ships across and into another sector of space to replace it as caregiver of a species. Meanwhile Janeway has to resolve problems with terrorists.

Enterprise Pilot: Archer deals with the distrust of Vulcans while returning a Klingon to it's home world. Meanwhile, Archer learns of a temporal cold war.

ST Discovery Pilot: Burnham is introduced as a "Half Sister" to a legendary Spock (whos lore and life is well established but ignored...because the script needs it to be). This new character, who was raised in a Vulcan home, mutinies against her Captain, initiates a war with the Klingons resulting in her Captain being killed, is court-martialed and imprisoned for life.

ST Academy: A several hundred year old Captain Ake emotionally manipulates a criminal into joining her crew on board the Academy ship. Meanwhile some bullshit happens resulting in the Criminal revealing to a enemy where Ake and her ship is. We're introduced to the villain who is holding a grudge against Ake.

We went from thoughtful and dramatic situations to just pure drama for the sake of drama. The writing of Kurtzman and his people is simply terrible and problematic at best. They repeatedly disrespect the established lore and publicly state their version of Trek is not for "us" the "old Trek fans". F*ck this guy.

replies.author: @soaringeagle5418
replies.text: @lukehahn4489 Its still a scam though. RT is owned by Fandango, which is primarily owned by Versant (a company spun off from NBCUniversal/Comcast) with a minority stake held by Warner Bros. Discovery. To leave a "verified" review you have to purchase your ticket through Fandango but people have sold their "verified reviews" after purchasing a ticket. Its possible to purchase a ticket through fanadango, not go to the theater, and still leave a verified review (cough Captain Marvel cough).  (The verification happens from purchasing the ticket not using it). Many independent theaters (like the one in my town) don't offer ticket sales through fandango for several reasons. Fandango charges users a convenience fee that increases the ticket cost to movie goers. That fee is non-refundable even for events beyond the purchaser's control. Small theaters that use fandango can take anywhere up to 30 days to receive the funds. AMC has a partnership with fandango and gets a better deal than other theater chains.

author: @robertanderson6929
text:
When I think about sitting in the theater watching the previews before _Cloverfield 2008_ and seeing the teaser trailer for _Star Trek 2009_ it makes me sad.  The teaser was so good.  It was just a montage of voice overs from the space program and pictures of gritty iron workers welding up something enormous when suddenly the unfinished saucer section of the Enterprise NCC-1701 came into view and Spock's voice saying, "Space, the final frontier."

This was a time before social media.  I had no idea who J.J. Abrams was.  I had no idea a movie was even in the works.  I cannot in words express the height of my excitement and anticipation.  But to look back now after 15 years all I feel is sadness over the squandered opportunity.  RIP Star Trek.  Perhaps some future generation will understand our loss.

author: @anadverb5063
text: For me, Star Trek is like a beloved, but now elderly and terminally ill dog. The thought of ending its life is painful beyond measure, but even more excruciating is having to watch my best friend as he suffers needlessly—not merely a shadow of his former self—but utterly unrecognizable as the sharp, effervescent, and beautiful creature brimming with both life and love that I  once knew. Try as I might, my love simply isn’t enough to restore what once was, and so, with a heavy heart, I have no choice but to recognize that “All Good Things” do indeed end, then act accordingly. The loss is crippling, but I also recognize that I am forever changed for the better by the beautiful soul that functioned as one of my greatest teachers. Farewell, Star Trek. I love you enough to let you go.

author: @bwill887
text: What I really want to know is how companies can continually get away with putting out costly shows that no one wants, watches, or cares about. Presumably, these shows are being issued by companies that want to make money at the support of a Board of Directors and shareholders who also want to make money. How Paramount continues to fund Kurtzman's vision despite the high costs, the losing and alienation of fans, and not getting a new fan base is a serious concern, to the point it borders or corporate malfeasance. These shows are expensive to put together and the company and its shareholders shouldn't be wanting to lose millions with each new show. To continue to let a team produce material with the same vision no one wanta is not acting in anyone's interest, be it the company, the investors, and maybe even the production team if that effort can be put somewhere else. I cannot understand how leadership changes weren't made before the cancelation of the entire franchise seems like a prudent response. Unless there is something in the contract requiring a certain number of releases over a time period (and even if that is the case, the insanity to keep trying the same thing while hoping for a different result), the company's actions should be questioned by its investors.

The writers in those "kids" shows demonstrated a love for and deep knowledge of the worlds the stories were set it, and treated their audiences as not stupid, and didn't spend a zillion dollars telling their stories.

text: Maybe the problem is - who are the writers now? How many have real world experience as opposed to fast tracking through a modern unilithic college English Department? They constituted a broad cross section of society and shared hardships, combined with a quality primary, secondary, and college education.  They experienced things that gave reason to reflect on a deeper plane, and possessed a broad liberal education that provided the technical writing skills to deliver their ruminations to a shared audience.  How many war veterans or blue collar rooted folks are attracted to writing these days? How many would be welcomed to a modern college writing program. The characters and stories of Star Trek Star Fleet Academy accurately reflects the type of writers prevalent today.

replies.author: @jaketheripper7385
replies.text:
​​@g.v4848 That's the thing, though...  "The Clone Wars" was actually really solid on its own and didn't need to rely so heavily on nostalgia to be good or draw an audience.  It had everything to appeal to older fans while still doing enough new to bring in a younger audience and create a new generation of fans.  It was made by people who actually loved and appreciated Star Wars, but who wanted to improve on the prequels.  "The Clone Wars" is great because it can still stand on its own even without the prequels which often feel like more of a supplement to "The Clone Wars" than the other way around.  They both compliment one another while still being their own thing.  

Unfortunately, this momentum didn't really continue into "Rebels" or "The Bad Batch".  They're both fine, I guess, but "Rebels" feels much more like a kid's show while shoe-horning in certain elements to try to appease older fans, so it makes the entire series much more jarring and inconsistent in tone while coming off as cheesy and cartoonish.  It's just got Disney's fingerprints all over it...  "The Bad Batch" had potential and was arguably quite a bit better, but it still had its issues.  To me, it felt like it was starting to meander a bit before suddenly being forced to bring everything to a swift conclusion that ended up leaving quite bit unresolved and unexplored.  It seemed like they had so many more plans to expand on certain ideas and character elements, but they were forced to abandon all of that to shove a conclusion in at the very end because they ended up being told that they weren't getting another season, so they had to rework everything at the last minute to offer some kind of closure.  Honestly, I'm not even sure what happened behind the scenes with that whole situation because I've never cared enough about the show to look into it, but it feels like it suffered from sudden and last-minute production changes to me (although I could be wrong).

replies.author: @gormold4163
replies.text:
@meekrob this is a classic example of the importance of vocabulary when trying to speak from a position of authority. The article is trying to make official statements about how the show is not bad and it is just the usual suspects hating without thinking. He failed in part because rather than saying that they reflexively (as in immediately and without thought) review bombed, he said they reflectively (acting in a thoughtful, quiet, or contemplative manner, often while looking back on past events or pondering deeply) review bombed the show. This may be more in line with reality, but is almost a total inversion of what the article seems to intend to say.
Ironically it indicates that the one who was acting reflexively was the one who wrote the article, not necessarily the ones who review bombed the show.

author: @leroystea8069
text:
A long time ago, in a place called Las Vegas, there was a thing established by Paramount Parks.  It was in the Las Vegas Hilton which is now The Westgate Hotel and ran from 1998 to 2008. Thus was Star Trek The Experience.  

The setting was Deep Space Nine, Quark's Bar and the Admiral's Store filled with wonderful and expensive Trek items.  it has the historical Star Trek Museum, where gigantic models of TNG's Enterprise, USS Voyager, Kirk's Enterprise and even a Klingon Cruiser all hanging from a ceiling of painted universes and Trek music playing in the background.  You could "beam" aboard the TNG Enterprise, visit the bridge and ride a fantastic shuttle craft to reset the timeline in that story.  The Beaming effect was amazingly real for a fictional show.  Walls changed from Las Vegas to the transporter room.  There were Klingons and Ferangi there too.  

And by the time the whole thing began to close for good, due to a drop in admission audiences....it was then it felt like Star Trek had died.

Seeing this franchise being treated the way it is now, is for sure very sad.  Just like the closing of the Trek Experience.  It leaves a hole in your heart.
Only parts of it still exist.  Part of the drinking bar, engine pieces hanging from the ceiling.  There are videos about this place here on YT.  
But it is a shadow of what was once great.  
Hennessy Cognac is my drink of choice.  Here's to you Star Trek....damnit you were that good♥

author: @robertjenkins3308
text:
I was idly going through my old "The Best of Trek" paperbacks this weekend.  For those who don't know these, they are a bunch of articles originally written by fans for the old "Trek" fanzine which was published from the late 1970's through the early 1990s.  They examine many different aspects of TOS, TNG, and the first six films, with a little bit of DS9 and VOY also.  Everything from character analyses to parody articles to philosophical musings and much more.  And the articles are almost universally well-written and thought-provoking.  It's a great snapshot of the fandom of Trek in those days.

Can anyone even remotely imagine Abrams or Kurtzman Trek inspiring anything even remotely like this?  Me neither.

Hollywood used to be run by people who had done something else first. Roddenberry had been a soldier and a cop before he produced TV; Dick Wolf of Law and Order Fame was clearly an attorney; Vince Gilligan didn’t become a TV writer until well into his 30s. But these kids come straight from Ivy League safe spaces into Hollywood writing rooms with zero real human experience. Of course, what they write is like origami chicken—lightweight, tasteless and one dimensional.
